verses.text: And Samuel died; and all the Israelites were gathered together, and lamented him, and buried him in his house at Ramah. And David arose, and went down to the wilderness of Paran. Guidance: A single verse marks the end of an era. Samuel — who had judged Israel for decades, who anointed Saul and David, who functioned as the bridge between the age of judges and the age of kings — dies. 'All the Israelites' gather and lament: the public mourning rituals for a national figure, probably lasting the full seven days of formal grief. He is buried in his house at Ramah, which sounds odd to modern readers but was actually a common Israelite practice for venerated household-tomb burials. The death is reported with arresting brevity, the way Scripture often reports the deaths even of major figures — Moses gets one chapter, Joshua a half-chapter; Samuel here gets eight words. The most important men in a generation are not the most important on the page of history; God closes their chapters quickly. David, meanwhile, moves down to the wilderness of Paran (or possibly Maon, as some manuscripts read; the geography is consistent with the southern Judean wilderness). The man Samuel anointed is now on his own. The story will continue without Samuel ever appearing alive again.

And there was a man in Maon, whose possessions were in Carmel; and the man was very great, and he had three thousand sheep, and a thousand goats: and he was shearing his sheep in Carmel. Now the name of the man was Nabal; and the name of his wife Abigail: and she was a woman of good understanding, and of a beautiful countenance: but the man was churlish and evil in his doings; and he was of the house of Caleb. Guidance: Introduction of the chapter's two new characters, in stark juxtaposition. Nabal lives in Maon (just south of Hebron) with his estate at Carmel (the Judean Carmel, not the northern one), three thousand sheep and a thousand goats — by any measure, fabulously wealthy. He is also descended from Caleb, the great Joshua-era hero whose faith brought him this very land as inheritance. The descendant has the inheritance and none of the faith. Then the contrast: 'his wife Abigail' — 'of good understanding, and of a beautiful countenance.' Two virtues, intelligence (sekel — the same Spirit-given competence the narrator used of David in 18:5) and beauty. The narrator does not call her merely pretty; she is wise. 'But the man was churlish and evil in his doings' — Hebrew literally 'hard and evil in his dealings.' The name 'Nabal' itself means 'fool' in Hebrew (Prov 17:21 — 'he that begetteth a fool doeth it to his sorrow'). One scarcely believes anyone would name a son this; most likely he was nicknamed thus by people fed up with him, and the name stuck so completely it became his legal name. Abigail is married to a man whose proverbial folly is so well-known the kingdom calls him 'Fool' to his face.

And David heard in the wilderness that Nabal did shear his sheep. And David sent out ten young men, and David said unto the young men, Get you up to Carmel, and go to Nabal, and greet him in my name: And thus shall ye say to him that liveth in prosperity, Peace be both to thee, and peace be to thine house, and peace be unto all that thou hast. And now I have heard that thou hast shearers: now thy shepherds which were with us, we hurt them not, neither was there ought missing unto them, all the while they were in Carmel. Ask thy young men, and they will shew thee. Wherefore let the young men find favour in thine eyes: for we come in a good day: give, I pray thee, whatsoever cometh to thine hand unto thy servants, and to thy son David. Guidance: Sheep-shearing was a festal occasion in ancient Israel — the wool harvest produced major income, and the celebrations were notoriously generous (sheep-shearings in Genesis and 2 Samuel are repeatedly the settings of major dramatic moments). David sends ten young men — not two or three, the size of a friendly visit, but ten, the size of an honor delegation. The instructions are courteous to the point of elegance. Three benedictions of shalom: peace to you, peace to your house, peace to all you have. The greeting is the full Hebrew shalom-formula. Then the appeal: David's men have been a 'wall' around Nabal's shepherds (v16 will confirm this — they protected them from raiders during pasturing), and now David asks Nabal to reciprocate with provisions for the festival. The language is studiously deferential — 'find favor in thine eyes,' 'we come in a good day' (i.e., a festival day when generosity is expected), 'thy son David' (David, the king-in-waiting, addresses himself as 'son' to make it small). The request is legitimate and the form is impeccable. Nabal's response will be the chapter's tone-setting outrage.

And when David's young men came, they spake to Nabal according to all those words in the name of David, and ceased. And Nabal answered David's servants, and said, Who is David? and who is the son of Jesse? there be many servants now a days that break away every man from his master. Shall I then take my bread, and my water, and my flesh that I have killed for my shearers, and give it unto men, whom I know not whence they be? Guidance: 'Who is David? and who is the son of Jesse?' The dismissal is both rude and politically loaded. Nabal pretends not to know — but David's reputation has been national since chapter 17, and a Calebite landowner in Judah cannot plausibly never have heard of the most famous Judahite alive. The pretended ignorance is contempt. 'There be many servants now a days that break away every man from his master' — Nabal categorizes David as a runaway slave. He is taking Saul's official line — 'David is a rebel against his master' — and using it as the political cover for his refusal. 'My bread, my water, my flesh' — three uses of the possessive in one sentence; Nabal cannot give without naming what is his. He has not understood that the very flocks producing the wool he is shearing were kept safe by the men he is now refusing to feed. Every man is a fool who imagines his prosperity is self-produced. Wealth that does not remember its protectors becomes a closed loop of self-congratulation, until the day comes when the protectors stop protecting. Nabal is one insult away from that day arriving.

So David's young men turned their way, and went again, and came and told him all those sayings. And David said unto his men, Gird ye on every man his sword. And they girded on every man his sword; and David also girded on his sword: and there went up after David about four hundred men; and two hundred abode by the stuff. Guidance: The young men return with the report. David's reaction is fury. 'Gird ye on every man his sword.' Four hundred men gear up; two hundred stay behind to guard the camp's supplies (the same proportion David uses in his later raids per 30:9-10). Note the contrast with the chapter that just preceded. In chapter 24, David spared the king who was actively trying to kill him. In chapter 25, David is about to massacre every male in the household of a man who insulted him at a festival. The same man — restraint toward the LORD's anointed, rage toward a private rude landowner. Why? Because chapter 24 had a theological framework ('the LORD's anointed') that bounded David's hand; chapter 25 does not. Without that frame, his fury runs straight to the sword. The chapter is teaching us that even great hearts need a theology that reaches into every category of grievance. Restraint in the political sphere does not automatically translate into restraint in the personal. David is about to be saved from his own sword by a woman riding down the mountain road.

But one of the young men told Abigail, Nabal's wife, saying, Behold, David sent messengers out of the wilderness to salute our master; and he railed on them. But the men were very good unto us, and we were not hurt, neither missed we any thing, as long as we were conversant with them, when we were in the fields: They were a wall unto us both by night and day, all the while we were with them keeping the sheep. Now therefore know and consider what thou wilt do; for evil is determined against our master, and against all his household: for he is such a son of Belial, that a man cannot speak to him. Guidance: An anonymous young servant — perhaps an under-shepherd who had served alongside David's men in the pastures — goes to Abigail. The verse is one of the most beautiful sociology in the Old Testament. The lowest-status person in the household runs to the second-highest because he knows the second-highest is the only one who can act. The servant gives a precise factual report: (1) David's messengers came with peace; (2) Nabal screamed at them; (3) David's men were genuinely 'a wall unto us' — physical protection — for the whole pasturing season; (4) we never lost a sheep. Then the assessment: 'evil is determined against our master and all his household.' Then the diagnosis of why the servant cannot speak to Nabal himself: 'he is such a son of Belial, that a man cannot speak to him.' Nabal is the kind of man no one can correct. The household has organized itself around routing around the foolishness of its master. The servant turns to the only person in the house who might still reach reason: the wife. Sometimes God arranges that the lowest-paid person in a household is the one who saves it.

Then Abigail made haste, and took two hundred loaves, and two bottles of wine, and five sheep ready dressed, and five measures of parched corn, and an hundred clusters of raisins, and two hundred cakes of figs, and laid them on asses. And she said unto her servants, Go on before me; behold, I come after you. But she told not her husband Nabal. Guidance: Abigail moves. The inventory is staggering: 200 loaves, 2 large wineskins, 5 dressed sheep, 5 seahs (about 35 quarts) of parched grain, 100 raisin clusters, 200 fig cakes — enough food for David's six hundred men for several days. The detail of the list is the narrator's way of showing us how much Nabal could have spared and didn't. Abigail loads it on asses and sends the gift ahead. 'Go on before me; behold, I come after you' — she has the donkeys arrive at David first, so that he sees the provision before he sees the petitioner. Tactical genius. And the final line: 'but she told not her husband Nabal.' This is the same phrase used in 19:12 about Michal's escape of David from Saul. Abigail acts to save the household by going around the master who would have prevented the saving. The narrator does not editorialize on this; she does what needed doing. Sometimes the most loyal act available to a person bound to a fool is to act for the household's preservation without seeking the fool's permission.

And it was so, as she rode on the ass, that she came down by the covert of the hill, and, behold, David and his men came down against her; and she met them. Now David had said, Surely in vain have I kept all that this fellow hath in the wilderness, so that nothing was missed of all that pertained unto him: and he hath requited me evil for good. So and more also do God unto the enemies of David, if I leave of all that pertain to him by the morning light any that pisseth against the wall. Guidance: The geography is precise: Abigail descends on her donkey through a covered ravine ('the covert of the hill' — a hidden defile of the trail), and David is coming up the same trail in the opposite direction. They meet at the bend. The narrator flashes back to David's vow on the way down: he has been muttering to himself the whole march. 'Surely in vain have I kept all that this fellow hath' — the bitterness is real. He had protected Nabal's flocks and gotten contempt in return. The vow is brutal: 'if I leave of all that pertain to him by the morning light any that pisseth against the wall' — a coarse Hebrew expression for every male; David has vowed to kill every male in Nabal's household by morning. This is the sentence Abigail is about to interrupt. David's vow is the kind of vow people make in the heat of an outrage and regret later; the man is at his most dangerous in this moment. The narrator records the vow verbatim so that we can see how close David was to a crime he would have spent the rest of his life trying to atone for. Some of God's most precious mercies are the unfulfilled vows He arranges to have interrupted.

And it was so, as she rode on the ass, that she came down by the covert of the hill, and, behold, David and his men came down against her; and she met them. Now David had said, Surely in vain have I kept all that this fellow hath in the wilderness, so that nothing was missed of all that pertained unto him: and he hath requited me evil for good. So and more also do God unto the enemies of David, if I leave of all that pertain to him by the morning light any that pisseth against the wall. Guidance: The geography is precise: Abigail descends on her donkey through a covered ravine ('the covert of the hill' — a hidden defile of the trail), and David is coming up the same trail in the opposite direction. They meet at the bend. The narrator flashes back to David's vow on the way down: he has been muttering to himself the whole march. 'Surely in vain have I kept all that this fellow hath' — the bitterness is real. He had protected Nabal's flocks and gotten contempt in return. The vow is brutal: 'if I leave of all that pertain to him by the morning light any that pisseth against the wall' — a coarse Hebrew expression for every male; David has vowed to kill every male in Nabal's household by morning. This is the sentence Abigail is about to interrupt. David's vow is the kind of vow people make in the heat of an outrage and regret later; the man is at his most dangerous in this moment. The narrator records the vow verbatim so that we can see how close David was to a crime he would have spent the rest of his life trying to atone for. Some of God's most precious mercies are the unfulfilled vows He arranges to have interrupted.

And it was so, as she rode on the ass, that she came down by the covert of the hill, and, behold, David and his men came down against her; and she met them. So and more also do God unto the enemies of David, if I leave of all that pertain to him by the morning light any that pisseth against the wall. They meet at the bend. The narrator flashes back to David's vow on the way down: he has been muttering to himself the whole march. 'Surely in vain have I kept all that this fellow hath' — the bitterness is real. He had protected Nabal's flocks and gotten contempt in return. The vow is brutal: 'if I leave of all that pertain to him by the morning light any that pisseth against the wall' — a coarse Hebrew expression for every male; David has vowed to kill every male in Nabal's household by morning. This is the sentence Abigail is about to interrupt. David's vow is the kind of vow people make in the heat of an outrage and regret later; the man is at his most dangerous in this moment. The narrator records the vow verbatim so that we can see how close David was to a crime he would have spent the rest of his life trying to atone for. Some of God's most precious mercies are the unfulfilled vows He arranges to have interrupted.

And when Abigail saw David, she hasted, and lighted off the ass, and fell before David on her face, and bowed herself to the ground, And fell at his feet, and said, Upon me, my lord, upon me let this iniquity be: and let thine handmaid, I pray thee, speak in thine audience, and hear the words of thine handmaid. Let not my lord, I pray thee, regard this man of Belial, even Nabal: for as his name is, so is he; Nabal is his name, and folly is with him: but I thine handmaid saw not the young men of my lord, whom thou didst send. Guidance: Abigail dismounts, falls flat on her face, and offers her opening line: 'Upon me, my lord, upon me let this iniquity be.' She takes responsibility before David can name an accusation. The Hebrew construction is intensive; she is volunteering to absorb the blame entirely. Then she asks permission to speak, in the most deferential possible way: 'let thine handmaid, I pray thee, speak in thine audience, and hear the words of thine handmaid.' Two requests for hearing in one sentence — a rhetorical pattern that allows David's anger to subside while she opens with non-threatening words. Then she names her husband honestly: 'this man of Belial, even Nabal: for as his name is, so is he; Nabal is his name, and folly is with him.' She does not defend Nabal; she names him. She tells David that the contempt was the master's, not the household's — 'I thine handmaid saw not the young men of my lord, whom thou didst send.' She had not been consulted. The argument is direct: punish the right person, who is not her household. Abigail is one of the great rhetorical performances in scripture; she is doing in front of a man with a drawn sword what most of us could not do at our calmest.

Now therefore, my lord, as the LORD liveth, and as thy soul liveth, seeing the LORD hath withholden thee from coming to shed blood, and from avenging thyself with thine own hand, now let thine enemies, and they that seek evil to my lord, be as Nabal. And now this blessing which thine handmaid hath brought unto my lord, let it even be given unto the young men that follow my lord. I pray thee, forgive the trespass of thine handmaid: for the LORD will certainly make my lord a sure house; because my lord fighteth the battles of the LORD, and evil hath not been found in thee all thy days. Yet a man is risen to pursue thee, and to seek thy soul: but the soul of my lord shall be bound in the bundle of life with the LORD thy God; and the souls of thine enemies, them shall he sling out, as out of the middle of a sling. Guidance: Abigail's speech is one of the most theologically sophisticated speeches by any woman in scripture. Three moves. (1) 'The LORD hath withholden thee from coming to shed blood, and from avenging thyself with thine own hand' — she diagnoses what is actually happening in the moment as the LORD's act, not hers. She is telling David that he is being saved from a sin in real time. (2) 'Let thine enemies be as Nabal' — she predicts Nabal's downfall without requiring David to be its agent. She is suggesting that God will deal with Nabal in His own way, freeing David's hand. (3) The promise: 'the LORD will certainly make my lord a sure house; because my lord fighteth the battles of the LORD.' She names what no one else in the chapter has — that David's kingdom is coming. Then comes the great line: 'the soul of my lord shall be bound in the bundle of life with the LORD thy God.' The phrase 'bundle of life' (tseror ha-chayyim) became, in later Jewish tradition, a standard funerary blessing — the souls of the righteous bound up in God's safekeeping. Abigail invents the phrase. 'And the souls of thine enemies, them shall he sling out, as out of the middle of a sling.' She uses the very imagery of David's most famous victory (the sling against Goliath) to describe what God will do with David's enemies. She is reading David back to himself through the lens of God's own protection. Faith in this moment looks like recalibrating someone's view of his own life by reminding him of what God has already done and is determined to do. There are days when a heart needs to be slowed by being shown its own theology spoken back to it.

And it shall come to pass, when the LORD shall have done to my lord according to all the good that he hath spoken concerning thee, and shall have appointed thee ruler over Israel; That this shall be no grief unto thee, nor offence of heart unto my lord, either that thou hast shed blood causeless, or that my lord hath avenged himself: but when the LORD shall have dealt well with my lord, then remember thine handmaid. Guidance: Abigail looks forward to David's kingship — a future she names with calm confidence — and makes the long-range case. 'When the LORD shall have appointed thee ruler over Israel' — she does not say 'if'; she says 'when.' The kingdom is settled. Then the prudential argument: do not give yourself a future grievance. 'That this shall be no grief unto thee, nor offence of heart unto my lord, either that thou hast shed blood causeless, or that my lord hath avenged himself.' She is asking David to picture himself ten years from now, on the throne, looking back on this afternoon — and feeling either the freedom of having spared a foolish man's household, or the heaviness of having slaughtered them in fury. She names what a lifetime of regret feels like. Many sins are sins you can talk yourself out of in the moment; few are sins you can talk yourself into being free of for the rest of your life. Abigail is asking David to vote with his future. 'When the LORD shall have dealt well with my lord, then remember thine handmaid.' She closes with a small personal request — that he remember her. Even her own preservation is named last, after his.

And David said to Abigail, Blessed be the LORD God of Israel, which sent thee this day to meet me: And blessed be thy advice, and blessed be thou, which hast kept me this day from coming to shed blood, and from avenging myself with mine own hand. For in very deed, as the LORD God of Israel liveth, which hath kept me back from hurting thee, except thou hadst hasted and come to meet me, surely there had not been left unto Nabal by the morning light any that pisseth against the wall. Guidance: David's response is one of the most beautiful triple-blessings in scripture: 'Blessed be the LORD… blessed be thy advice… blessed be thou.' He blesses God first (who sent the messenger), then her advice (the content of the rescue), then her person (the agent). The order is precise — gratitude to God for the means, gratitude for the wisdom delivered, gratitude for the woman who delivered it. Then the candid confession: 'thou hast kept me this day from coming to shed blood, and from avenging myself with mine own hand.' He acknowledges what he was about to do. He repeats the truth Abigail named: this was avenging with his own hand — the very sin he had explicitly refused in chapter 24. He had been the man-after-God's-own-heart toward the king who hunted him and almost a butcher toward a Calebite landowner who insulted him. The asymmetry could have ended his moral standing. Then David swears: 'as the LORD liveth… except thou hadst hasted and come to meet me, surely there had not been left unto Nabal by the morning light any that pisseth against the wall.' He confirms the vow he was on his way to fulfill. Abigail's intervention saved a household and saved David. The chapter is the picture of how God uses other people to keep us from becoming the worst version of ourselves we were prepared to become.

So David received of her hand that which she had brought him; and said unto her, Go up in peace to thine house; see, I have hearkened to thy voice, and have accepted thy person. Guidance: David accepts the gift. The acceptance functions as a kind of treaty — the matter is closed; Nabal's household will not be destroyed. 'Go up in peace to thine house.' He sends her home with the same word she had carried — peace. 'See, I have hearkened to thy voice' — the verb shama (to listen, to hear, to obey) is a deliberate choice; David is saying he has not just heard her but has been moved by her counsel. 'I have accepted thy person' — literally 'lifted up thy face,' the formula for showing favor. The encounter has been transformative on both sides. Abigail rides home, having accomplished what she set out to do. David turns his army around and returns to camp without the bloodshed he was minutes from committing. The chapter has reached its hinge; everything that follows is the consequences of the rescue, not the rescue itself. God's mercy sometimes shows up wearing the most ordinary disguise — a wife on a donkey with figs and raisins — and the disguise is what makes the mercy effective. The rescuer who looks like rescue cannot reach the angry man; the rescuer who looks like a respectful gift-bearing wife can.

And Abigail came to Nabal; and, behold, he held a feast in his house, like the feast of a king; and Nabal's heart was merry within him, for he was very drunken: wherefore she told him nothing, less or more, until the morning light. But it came to pass in the morning, when the wine was gone out of Nabal, and his wife had told him these things, that his heart died within him, and he became as a stone. And it came to pass about ten days after, that the LORD smote Nabal, that he died. Guidance: Abigail returns home to find Nabal hosting a 'feast like the feast of a king' — the irony is unmistakable. The man who refused to share a portion with David's six hundred is throwing a royal-scale party for himself. 'Nabal's heart was merry within him, for he was very drunken' — too drunk to receive serious news. Abigail, with the same operational wisdom that saved the household in the morning, holds the report until the wine wears off. The next morning, she tells him everything. 'His heart died within him, and he became as a stone.' Likely a stroke from the shock of realizing how close to death he had been. He lingers as an incapacitated invalid for ten days, and then 'the LORD smote Nabal, that he died.' The narrator gives the death its theological subject: not 'Nabal died' but 'the LORD smote Nabal.' Abigail's prediction in v26 — 'let thine enemies be as Nabal' — is fulfilled in the only way that did not require David's hand. God settled the matter. The story is one of the cleanest illustrations in scripture of Romans 12:19 written a millennium in advance: 'Avenge not yourselves… for it is written, Vengeance is mine; I will repay, saith the Lord.' Vengeance arrived precisely; it just took ten days and required no one's sword.

But it came to pass in the morning, when the wine was gone out of Nabal, and his wife had told him these things, that his heart died within him, and he became as a stone. And it came to pass about ten days after, that the LORD smote Nabal, that he died. Guidance: Abigail returns home to find Nabal hosting a 'feast like the feast of a king' — the irony is unmistakable. The man who refused to share a portion with David's six hundred is throwing a royal-scale party for himself. 'Nabal's heart was merry within him, for he was very drunken' — too drunk to receive serious news. Abigail, with the same operational wisdom that saved the household in the morning, holds the report until the wine wears off. The next morning, she tells him everything. 'His heart died within him, and he became as a stone.' Likely a stroke from the shock of realizing how close to death he had been. He lingers as an incapacitated invalid for ten days, and then 'the LORD smote Nabal, that he died.' The narrator gives the death its theological subject: not 'Nabal died' but 'the LORD smote Nabal.' Abigail's prediction in v26 — 'let thine enemies be as Nabal' — is fulfilled in the only way that did not require David's hand. God settled the matter. The story is one of the cleanest illustrations in scripture of Romans 12:19 written a millennium in advance: 'Avenge not yourselves… for it is written, Vengeance is mine; I will repay, saith the Lord.' Vengeance arrived precisely; it just took ten days and required no one's sword.

And when David heard that Nabal was dead, he said, Blessed be the LORD, that hath pleaded the cause of my reproach from the hand of Nabal, and hath kept his servant from evil: for the LORD hath returned the wickedness of Nabal upon his own head. And David sent and communed with Abigail, to take her to him to wife. Guidance: David's response to the news is a three-fold blessing of the LORD: (1) for pleading David's cause (the legal language is striking — God as advocate, not just punisher); (2) for keeping David from evil (David is grateful that the LORD prevented his own sin); (3) for returning Nabal's wickedness on his own head (God's justice landed precisely where it belonged). The verse is a model of how to receive vindication: bless God for the rescue, bless God for keeping you from sinning, bless God for the precision of His justice. Then, in the same breath, David sends word to Abigail asking her to marry him. The brevity is striking. The wisdom and grace that saved his moral life are also the wisdom and grace he wants in his household. He has just lost his first wife Michal to Saul's politics (she was given to Phaltiel; see v44). He wants Abigail. There is a category of relationship in which the person who saved you from your worst self becomes, eventually, the partner of your best self. David recognizes the gift God just sent and does not waste it.

And when the servants of David were come to Abigail to Carmel, they spake unto her, saying, David sent us unto thee, to take thee to him to wife. And she arose, and bowed herself on her face to the earth, and said, Behold, let thine handmaid be a servant to wash the feet of the servants of my lord. And Abigail hasted, and arose, and rode upon an ass, and five damsels of hers that went after her; and she went after the messengers of David, and became his wife. Guidance: David's marriage delegation arrives at Carmel. Abigail's response is breathtaking in its humility: 'Let thine handmaid be a servant to wash the feet of the servants of my lord.' She does not just accept; she offers to be a foot-washing slave to David's slaves. Foot-washing was the lowest task in an ancient Near-Eastern household; she places herself below the bottom rung. Why? Because she sees what David is — the LORD's anointed king-in-waiting — and she does not assume that her own gifts entitle her to anything but service. The same wisdom that saved her household by speaking up now saves her marriage by bowing low. Abigail's posture is the posture of a person who has internalized the central principle of David's own life: the kingdom is not yours to grasp; God is the one who exalts the humble. She rides off with her five maidservants behind her, leaves Carmel forever, and becomes David's wife. She will go with him through the rest of the wilderness years, the Ziklag captivity, the move to Hebron, and the establishment of his throne. The wise woman whose marriage to a fool ended in her widowhood becomes the wise wife of the king of Israel. Sometimes God's faithfulness in the form of a hard marriage is preparing the wisdom needed for a different kind of partnership a few months later.

David also took Ahinoam of Jezreel; and they were also both of them his wives. But Saul had given Michal his daughter, David's wife, to Phalti the son of Laish, which was of Gallim. Guidance: Two footnotes that close the chapter and re-orient the household chess pieces. (1) David also married Ahinoam of Jezreel (probably the southern Judean Jezreel, not the famous northern one), so he is now two-wived: Abigail and Ahinoam. The narrator reports it without judgment; polygamy was practiced in the period, even though Deut 17:17 warned future kings against multiplying wives. The reader is left to notice that David, like every Israelite king to come, is already accumulating wives. The seeds of later marital troubles (Bathsheba, the household chaos of 2 Samuel) are being planted in this very chapter. (2) Meanwhile, Saul has taken Michal — David's first wife — and given her to a man named Phalti son of Laish from Gallim. The act was political (severing David's legal connection to the royal family) and probably also vindictive (Michal had once helped David escape; this is her father punishing her). Phalti's heartbreak when Michal is later returned to David in 2 Sam 3:15-16 — 'her husband went with her along weeping behind her' — shows that this was a real marriage. Saul has now used his daughter as a weapon twice in the David saga. The chapter closes with David's house growing and Saul's house unraveling. The kingdom is changing hands not just on battlefields but in marriage chambers. The political weight of who is whose spouse will increasingly shape the rest of 1 and 2 Samuel. We have just watched, in a single chapter, the death of Samuel, the death of Nabal, the saving of David's character by Abigail, and the political mutilation of David's first marriage. The wilderness years are now reshaping not just David's location but his entire household.
